data.title: Micron Technology On Pace For Record High Close — Data Talk
data.description:
Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) is currently at $154.70, up $4.13 or 2.74% 




     --Would be new all-time high (Based on available data back to June 1, 1984) 



     --Would be the first record close since June 18, 2024, when it closed at $153.45 



     --Currently up eight consecutive days; up 30.57% over this period 



     --Longest winning streak since June 12, 2025, when it rose for nine straight trading days 



     --Best eight day stretch since the eight days ending April 1, 2024, when it rose 32.23% 



     --Up 17.76% this week; best weekly performance since the week ending Sept. 27, 2024, when it rose 18.26% 



     --Up 29.99% month-to-date

data.title: Low-cost index funds are best for investors, right? Not so fast.
data.description:
By Brett Arends

New ETF aims to beat the S&P 500 just by avoiding its obvious blunders

Wait, what? Many index funds have a flaw.

When it comes to investing your retirement or college funds for the long term, few principles are now as widely accepted as the merits of low-cost index funds.

Most big investment firms have now followed pioneer Vanguard and offer investors a choice of funds that make no pretense of trying to pick individual stocks or bonds, but just simply follow the overall "indexes" passively, typically for a very low fee.

It's the rationale behind the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust SPY and legions of others.

Index funds, goes the theory, are the best option because they charge so little and produce the best long-term investment results, handily beating alternatives almost all the time.

But now comes remarkable new research that says that conventional wisdom may be flawed. While, yes, index funds will usually beat higher-fee "actively managed" funds, they may not be the optimal investment structure after all. On the contrary, many have a hidden flaw, and it's bigger than anyone realized.

In total, that flaw may be costing ordinary investors in the likes of an S&P 500 stock index fund a staggering 0.69 percentage points or more a year. Over time, this is huge.

The flaw has been exposed, and quantified, by legendary fund strategist Rob Arnott, chairman of Research Affiliates, and his colleague Lillian Wu in a new paper.

The flaw: Those index funds don't just "passively" pick stocks, even though everyone assumes they do. That's because, in order to maintain their index, they routinely drop falling stocks from the list and add rising ones. (This is why blue-chip indexes today include newcomers like Alphabet (GOOG)( GOOGL), Meta (META) and Tesla (TSLA) and not the giants of yesteryear like John D. Rockefeller's Standard Oil or Cornelius Vanderbilt's New York and Harlem Railroad.) For large-cap indexes such as the S&P 500 SPX and the Russell 1000 RUI, the changes amount to about 5% a year.

Everybody knows this happens, but figures the costs to index-fund owners are small.

They're wrong.

"Our research has shown that additions to the S&P 500 frequently beat the market ahead of their inclusion - only to lag behind in the year that follows," wrote Arnott and Wu. The index funds buy these stocks after they have risen far enough to merit inclusion in the index, and sell stocks after they have fallen so far they no longer merit inclusion. Buy high, sell low.

Even worse, they found, is that in many cases these stocks amount to flip-flops: The index buys a stock after it has risen - and then dumps it again after it has fallen.

"More than one-fourth of all stocks added to the S&P 500 or the Russell 1000 from 1989 through 2014 were dropped within 10 years," they reported. And well over half of all the stocks deleted from the indexes were added back again within 10 years.

Arkansas-based department-store chain Dillard's (DDS) has been in and out of the Russell 1000 RUI index so many times it needs a revolving door, Arnott said.

The costs of all this are astonishing.

"Addition flip-flops are truly awful for index-fund investors," Arnott and Wu wrote. ("Addition flip-flops" are stocks that are added to the index after rising, only to be dumped again within 10 years after falling again.) They beat the overall market index by an average of 75.6% in the year before they get included in the index. And as that happens before they are included, index-fund owners miss out on these gains completely. But then, once these former high-fliers were included in the index, they underperformed the market by an average of 69.2% before getting kicked out again. Oh, and after they get deleted again, on average they beat the market again, by 3.7% in the subsequent 12 months.

Ouch.

All of this takes place within the narrow 5% of your index fund that gets changed in any typical year to take account of rising and falling stocks. The other 95% of your fund, holding companies that hang around, is unaffected. As a result, Arnott and Wu argue, index-fund investors are effectively - if unknowingly - investing in two funds simultaneously: A low-cost passive fund that handles 95% of the money, and an active fund with 5% that resembles "an active emerging-growth manager on stimulants," constantly chasing recent performance, buying things after they have gone up and selling them after they have fallen. On average, the stocks being added to the index are four times as expensive, in relation to their fundamentals, as the stocks being sold.

The performance of that 5% fund is so awful that it drags down the performance of the index fund by meaningful amounts.

How much? Here's an illustration. Research Affiliates has come up with an alternative approach that is designed as far as possible to replicate the performance of that 95% and cut down on the damaging 5%. To do so, it involves business fundamental measures such as net assets, and five-year averages of sales, cash flow, dividends and stock buybacks in the decision of whether or not to include a stock in the index. These things move much more slowly than stock prices. This sharply cuts down on the amount of stocks you add or delete every year. Most importantly, it cuts down on the faddish flip-flops.

The result is what it calls the Research Affiliates Cap-Weighted Index, or RACWI.

Going as far back as 1991, they calculated that this strategy would have beaten the S&P 500 by an average of 0.69 percentage points a year. "From July 1991 to the end of 2024, RACWI beat S&P 500 by 69 bps [basis points - i.e., 0.69 percentage points] per annum," they said. "In just over three decades, an investor in RACWI U.S. would have accumulated approximately 23% more wealth than an investor in the S&P 500."

They added: "RACWI behaves almost identically to the S&P 500 in aggregate, but a handful of patient decisions at the margin - avoiding speculative growth stories until fundamentals validate investor enthusiasm, thereby avoiding frothy failures, and holding on to substantial businesses until fundamentals truly warrant their removal from the index-are what transform those small differences into meaningful long-term value."

Research Affiliates on Friday is launching a low-cost ETF, RAUS, that will pursue this strategy. The fees will be a low 0.15% a year, with the additional sweetener that at the start they will actually be 0%.

But what's most remarkable about this research is that all of this outperformance of RACWI, compared to regular index funds, just comes from that 5% that isn't being traded.

This means the losses borne by that 5% in a regular index fund are immense. If 5% of your portfolio is dragging down the whole thing by 0.69 percentage points a year, that 5% is losing nearly 14% a year.

Logically, this also means that whoever takes the other side of that trade - who buys the stocks being dumped by the regular indexes and sells the stuff they add - must be making good money.

The revelation of the hefty hidden costs of indexes is, or should be, big news. It's going to be interesting to see how the rest of the fund industry reacts. Meanwhile, this is a new strategy in the real world and a new ETF. It bears watching.

-Brett Arends

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-12-25 1019ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Winklevoss twins get their $2 billion payday as Gemini's stock gets set to trade
data.description:
By Steve Gelsi

Gemini's IPO prices well above the expected range, in a sign of strong investor demand for the crypto-trading company's stock

Tyler Winklevoss, left, and Cameron Winklevoss are taking their cryptocurrency-exchange company Gemini Space Station public amid strong interest in digital currencies.

Gemini Space Station Inc., the cryptocurrency exchange founded by the billionaire Winklevoss twins to bring cryptocurrencies to the masses, is set to start trading Friday as one of four IPOs in the busiest week for stock-market newcomers in years.

Tyler and Cameron Winklevoss, who once sued Meta Platforms (META) Chief Executive Mark Zuckerberg for allegedly stealing their idea for Facebook, are getting a payday of $2 billion, plus any gains in Gemini's stock after its debut.

The twins' knack for making money may soon be illustrated once again, as Gemini's stock (GEMI) priced at $28 a share for its trading debut on Friday. That's a sign of strong demand for the IPO given that it's going public $2 above its already upped price range of $24 to $26 a share.

Earlier this week, it had raised the IPOs estimated price range price from its earlier level of $17 to $19 a share.

With 15.18 million shares being offered for sale - fewer than the expected 16.67 million - Gemini's IPO raised about $425 million. Goldman Sachs and Citigroup are the lead underwriters for the deal.

The Winklevoss twins, through the Winklevoss Capital Fund LLC, own 75.37 million Gemini shares, with a value of $2.11 billion at the IPO price.

It's uncertain when Gemini's stock will begin trading on Friday. If it's any guide, when Klarna Group PLC (KLAR) went public on Wednesday, the first trade wasn't till around 1 p.m. Eastern.

Investors aren't just hot for Gemini's stock, or new crypto issues - Wall Street bankers are tapping into strong demand for new issues in general.

The Renaissance IPO exchange-traded fund IPO - a gauge of the IPO market that rebalances quarterly and holds stocks up to three years after their debut - has run up 20.2% in 2025 in recent morning trading Friday, well ahead of the S&P 500 index's SPX 12% gain.

After the long-awaited debut of buy-now-pay-later specialist Klarna on Wednesday, and after stablecoin and crypto-exchange company Figure Technology Solutions Inc. (FIGR) went public on Thursday, Gemini will highlight an unusually crowded coming-out party for four IPOs on Friday.

Along with Gemini, the other companies priced to trade on Friday's trading are heating and ventilation portfolio company Legence (LGN), coffee chain Black Rock Coffee Bar Inc. (BRCB), which both priced their IPOs late Thursday, and traffic-management company Via Transportation Inc. (VIA).

Renaissance Capital analyst Matthew Kennedy said earlier this week that this week's crop of IPOs marked the busiest for sizable IPOs - those that raise over $250 million - since 2021.

"They're launching into a period of robust demand for IPOs," Kennedy said.

Jay Ritter, a finance professor at the University of Florida, said it's rare to see four IPOs of $250 million or more debut on a Friday, attributing it to the Labor Day holiday on Sept 1.

Roadshows for prospective investors typically take several days, followed by pricing on Wednesday night for trading on a Thursday. But with a large number of deals waiting to go public right after the summer, the usual process was delayed by a day, he said.

"Roadshows usually start on Mondays," Ritter said. "This week was affected by the Labor Day holiday, which resulted in a Tuesday start for the marketing last week."

Overall, the IPO market has been fueled by a backlog of more mature companies that have grown bigger partly by raising money in private markets, as well as by a continued rally in the stock market, with the S&P 500 setting another record on Thursday.

Healthy interest in summertime IPOs from crypto firms such as Bullish (BLSH) and Circle Internet Group Inc. (CRCL) has also fueled interest in Gemini Space Station.

Gemini Space Station has generated a buzz, but it's not yet profitable

Gemini Space Station, the brainchild of Tyler and Cameron Winklevoss, who founded the company in 2014, is considered an early player in crypto trading.

"Crypto is a way out of the status quo dictated by traditional financial markets," Tyler and Cameron Winklevoss said in their IPO prospectus. "Gemini plans to be that full-service conduit that bridges the gap between traditional finance and crypto."

On that front, Gemini has recently rolled out the Gemini Credit Card, which offers cryptocurrency rewards on purchases.

The twins, who are former Harvard classmates with billionaire Mark Zuckerberg, were portrayed in the 2010 Oscar-winning movie "The Social Network," about the origins of Facebook, which later became Meta Platforms Inc. (META).

Their lawsuit against Meta founder and Chief Executive Zuckerberg, for allegedly stealing their idea for Facebook, won them a $65 million settlement in 2008.

They invested proceeds from their lawsuit into bitcoin (BTCUSD). By 2018, they were on the Forbes list of crypto billionaires. At last check, Forbes listed them as No. 341 on its list of the 400 richest Americans, with a combined net worth of $4.4 billion, which included their stake in Gemini Space Station.

Their fortunes could increase if Gemini's stock gains in its trading debut, as they continue to own 75.37 million shares in the company, with 94.5% of the company's voting power.

Kennedy, the Renaissance Capital analyst, said the Winklevoss connection to Gemini Space Station is getting attention.

"Just by virtue of the movie alone, they have some pretty broad name recognition," Kennedy said. "And name recognition can translate to some deal heat. Clearly, with the increased range, initial investors are betting on a hot open."

However, Kennedy urged investors to first consider the fundamentals for Gemini Space Station before moving to "own a new crypto company at any price," because "going forward, Gemini will really need to improve its bottom line."

Gemini reported an operating loss of $113.46 million and revenue of $68.6 million in the six months ending June 30. In the year-ago period, it lost $84.8 million on revenue of $74.3 million.

For full-year 2024, it reported a loss of $165.8 million on revenue of $142.2 million, and in 2023 it lost $311.6 million on revenue of $98.1 million.

One potential sweetener for investors in the IPO is a $50 million private placement in Gemini Space Station's stock by Nasdaq Inc. (NDAQ), since it's an established player in the exchange space.

"The bull case is Gemini turning its Nasdaq partnership into higher-margin prime and custody revenue; the bear case is whether they can scale that fast enough to overcome widening losses - either way, this listing reopens the window for crypto-adjacent IPOs," Maja Vujinovic, CEO of Digital Assets at FG Nexus, said in an email.

The interest in cryptocurrencies has also been fueled in part by President Donald Trump, who has launched his own coin.

"Firms like Gemini have a real opportunity to ride the IPO wave, if they launch strategically and show strong post-IPO performance," said Anthony Georgiades, general partner at Innovative Capital.

Legence IPO to tip the scales at $700 million

Legence, a specialist in heating, ventilation and air conditioning for data centers and other buildings, said Thursday it will offer 26 million shares at $28 a share, toward the top of its estimated price range of $25 to $29, raising $728 million.

Goldman Sachs and Jefferies are lead underwriters for the deal.

Blackstone Inc. (BX) acquired Therma Holdings LLC for an undisclosed sum in 2020 and renamed the company Legence in 2022.

With 103.65 million shares of Class A and Class B stock outstanding, San Jose, Calif.-based Legence has a market capitalization of about $2.9 billion.

Black Rock Coffee marks a rare entry from a consumer-facing business

The IPO from Black Rock Coffee marks a relatively rare entry from the retail food sector.

Scottsdale, Ariz.-based Black Rock Coffee offered 14.7 million shares of stock at $20 a share late Thursday, above its estimated price range of $16 to $18, raising $294 million.

J.P. Morgan, Jefferies and Morgan Stanley are the lead underwriters.

Founded in 2008 in Beaverton, Ore., Black Rock Coffee now operates at 158 locations in seven states.

With 48.19 million of Class A, Class B, and Class C common stock outstanding after the company goes public, Black Rock Coffee has a market capitalization of about $963.8 million.

Via Transportation readies IPO

Via Transportation, a New York-based maker of software for transportation-systems management, sold 10.7 million shares of stock at an IPO price of $46, which was above the estimated price range of $40 to $44 a share.

The IPO raised $492.86 million, with $164.29 million going to the company and $328.57 million to selling shareholders. The lead underwriters were Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, Allen & Co. and Wells Fargo Securities.

With 79.51 million shares outstanding after it goes public, Via has a market capitalization of $3.66 billion at the IPO price.

Mike Murphy and Frances Yue contributed

-Steve Gelsi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-12-25 1009ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: ICE Canola on the Rise in Early Trading
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Intercontinental Exchange canola futures were posting increases on Friday morning.

Support for the Canadian oilseed came from gains in Chicago soybeans and soyoil as well as European rapeseed. That was tempered by declines in Chicago soymeal and Malaysian palm oil.

Crude oil was higher, underpinning the vegetable oils. Also, canola was being guided by pre-report positioning ahead of Friday's supply and demand estimates from the U.S. Department of Agriculture.

Pressure on canola also came from the advancing harvest across the Prairies. Saskatchewan reported combining there was 41% complete with its canola at 12 % finished.

The Canadian dollar was virtually unchanged on Thursday morning, with the loonie at 72.21 U.S. cents compared to Thursday's close of 72.23.

Approximately 15,600 contracts were traded by 9:44 a.m. EDT and prices in Canadian dollars per metric ton were: 

 
               Price    Change 
Canola    Nov  636.50  up 4.80 
          Jan  647.90  up 3.80 
          Mar  659.30  up 3.80 
          May  669.30  up 3.70

Source: Commodity News Service Canada, news@marketsfarm.com 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 12, 2025 10:11 ET (14:11 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Some fear the Fed will follow Turkey's path to runaway inflation. How one strategist says investors can shield for that.
data.description:
By Jules Rimmer

Turkey's Recep Tayyip Erdogan and U.S. counterpart Donald Trump share a view of interest rates.

If the Fed under new management next year gives its employment mandate primacy over price control, letting inflation rip, then there's a danger the U.S. will "turn Turkish."

That's the warning from research house Gavekal, which predicts that equities SPX , crypto (BTCUSD) and gold (GC00) would thrive in such a scenario even as cash and bonds would suffer.

The parable of the Turkish central bank is a disturbing one. President Recep Tayyip Erdogan repeatedly sacked governors until he found one prepared to implement his "unorthodox" monetary policies and the end result was that the Turkish lira lost 93% of its value over the last decade (USDTRY), while inflation in Tukey soared into the triple digits.

For Gavekal strategist Will Denyer, something has to give in the U.S.: either stocks or bonds. At present, both exhibit characteristics of goldilocks, and pricing suggests that both growth and inflation will cool sufficiently for the Fed to relax monetary policy without the economy falling into recession. Denyer warns markets that they can't have it both ways.

Denyer's latest research note, published Friday, is headlined, "The risk of stagflation," and he emphasized that, on the evidence of recent data, "it would be a stretch to say the U.S. is already in stagflation ... but the direction of travel is worrying." This is because "the recent rate of jobs growth is usually only seen on the eve of recession," with an unemployment rate at 4.3% the highest since 2021. Meanwhile, inflation is now well above the Fed's target, no matter "how you measure it."

Inflation is moving further away from the Fed's target, however you measure it.

For Denyer, it's the rise in supercore service inflation that is most troubling because Fed Chair Jerome Powell himself has argued in the past that this is the best indication of underlying inflation trends.

So the Fed will convene next week, facing the thorny dilemma of conflicting directions for its dual mandate - controlling inflation and supporting jobs - and Denyer urges caution and restraint with just 25 basis points (FF00) of easing his recommendation. For Denyer, 2026 is likely to bring with it an inflationary boom as tax cuts from the budget bill begin to outweigh the fiscal drag of 2025's tariff imposition by U.S. President Donald Trump, who has expressed admiration in the past for Erdogan. This will happen at the same time global growth will be stimulated by fiscal easing in Japan, China and Europe.

MarketWatch First Take (November 2017): Trump's vision for America is Erdogan's Turkey

Denyer summarizes the predicament like this: If the U.S. succumbs to a deflationary recession, Treasurys will rally further, but if reflation prevails, bonds sell off and equities rise.

Given his hunch that stagflation is the likeliest outcome, Denyer backs the inflation (and dollar DXY ) hedges of crypto, gold and stocks, but right now, as uncertainty reigns, Gavekal's call is to use the concurrent rallies in bonds and stocks to lighten up on both in favor of T-bills BX:TMUBMUSD03M and international assets.

-Jules Rimmer

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-12-25 1001ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Consumer sentiment hits four-month low in September, lower and middle income consumers feel pinch
data.description:
The University of Michigan's gauge of consumer sentiment fell to a four-month low reading of 55.4 in September from 58.2 in the prior month.

The deterioration in sentiment was sharper than expected. Economists polled by the Wall Street Journal had expected sentiment would slip to 58.1 from 58.2 in the prior month.

The worsening of sentiment was particularly strong among lower and middle income consumers.

This is breaking news. Check back for updates.

-Greg Robb

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-12-25 1005ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Introducing Asti Week: A Sparkling Celebration Debuts Across 15 NYC Restaurants
data.description:
Introducing Asti Week: A Sparkling Celebration Debuts Across 15 NYC Restaurants

Introducing Asti Week: A Sparkling Celebration Debuts Across 15 NYC Restaurants

PR Newswire

NEW YORK, Sept. 12, 2025

(September 15–19, 2025)

NEW YORK, Sept. 12, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- This September marks the first edition of Asti Week in New York City—an exciting new initiative celebrating the elegance and versatility of Asti Spumante and Moscato d'Asti, two iconic sparkling wines from Piedmont, Italy.

Often reserved for dessert, these wines offer so much more. Asti Spumante, a fully sparkling wine, is known for its lively bubbles and fragrant notes of pear, acacia, and white flowers—perfect as an aperitivo or paired with savory fare. Moscato d'Asti, lower in alcohol and gently fizzy, offers a delicate sweetness with aromas of peach, sage, and orange blossom, and pairs beautifully with spicy dishes, salty cheeses, and even umami-rich foods.

From September 15th to 19th, fifteen of New York's most beloved restaurants will delight guests with a glass of Asti Spumante or a cocktail featuring it, along with a specially paired dish with Moscato d'Asti on the menu. The goal is to build greater awareness of the denomination and highlight how these wines can enhance a variety of dining moments—not just dessert.

"With Asti Week, we want to show New Yorkers—and the world—that Asti Spumante and Moscato d'Asti are not only joyful and approachable thanks to their low alcohol content, but also incredibly food-friendly wines," said Giacomo Pondini, Director of the Consorzio Asti DOCG. "This initiative is an invitation to rediscover our sparkling wines in new, and maybe unexpected, contexts."

Participating restaurants include: Amo, Cacio e Pepe, Creston Wine Bar, La Dong, Misirizzi, Osteria Carlina, Osteria Brooklyn @ LIC, San Carlo Osteria Piemonte, Palma, Pasta Eater, Pasta Eater Hell's Kitchen, Settepani, Terrone, The Leopard at Des Artistes, and Tarallucci e Vino.

Celebrate the debut of Asti Week, learn more about the Asti DOCG appellation and discover how these iconic bubbles can surprise, delight, and elevate any occasion.

Websites:

Consorzio Asti DOCG; Asti Week

For more information:

Virginia Cademartori, virginia@platinummedia.agency 

Arianna Restivo, arianna@platinummedia.agency 

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/introducing-asti-week-a-sparkling-celebration-debuts-across-15-nyc-restaurants-302555172.html

SOURCE Consorzio Asti DOCG

data.title: JackRabbit Expands Retail Footprint with DICK'S Sporting Goods, Public Lands, and DICK'S House of Sport
data.description:
JackRabbit Expands Retail Footprint with DICK'S Sporting Goods, Public Lands, and DICK'S House of Sport

JackRabbit Expands Retail Footprint with DICK'S Sporting Goods, Public Lands, and DICK'S House of Sport

PR Newswire

SAN DIEGO, Sept. 12, 2025

The beloved micro ebike and small EV brand brings its compact, all-terrain rides to more outdoor adventurers nationwide.

SAN DIEGO, Sept. 12, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- JackRabbit, the company redefining micromobility with ultra-light, portable small EVs and micro ebikes, is proud to announce its retail expansion into select DICK'S Sporting Goods, Public Lands, and DICK'S House of Sport locations, as well as online at dicks.com and publiclands.com.

Known for their compact design, all-terrain durability, and off-road power, JackRabbit's micro ebikes have become a go-to for outdoor enthusiasts, weekend recreationalists, and everyday riders looking for a smarter, more sustainable way to get around.

Now, two of the brand's most popular models, the JackRabbit OG2 and JackRabbit XG, will be available both in-store and online at participating DICK'S Sporting Goods, Public Lands, and DICK'S House of Sport locations across the U.S.

"This expansion makes it even easier for more folks to get their hands on a JackRabbit and experience the fun and versatility of our 100% electric micro ebikes," said Jason Kenagy, CEO of JackRabbit. "Athletes and adventurers have the same need: getting from here to there without the hassle. That's why JackRabbit fits right in at DICK'S Sporting Goods, Public Lands, and DICK'S House of Sport. We're the pre-game ride, the post-hike cruise, and the 'why take the car?' solution for everything in between."

To find a participating DICK'S Sporting Goods, Public Lands, or DICK'S House of Sport location near you, visit: https://jackrabbit.bike/pages/find-a-store

About JackRabbit

JackRabbit Mobility, Inc. is the developer of award-winning and category-creating premium micro ebikes and small EVs known for their ultra-lightweight, portable, useful and mod-able design.

Launched in their hometown of San Diego in 2022, JackRabbit has since become the leader in 100% electric micromobility products, created and defined the "Micro eBike" and "Small EV" segments, and has grown a community of 60,000+ avid fans, known as "Jackos."

With a team of tech industry veterans, JackRabbit takes a platform-based, technology-driven approach - designing efficient, low-maintenance products with patented engineering, continuous innovation, reliable backwards compatibility, and a commitment to sustainability.

Today, JackRabbit offers a fleet of small EVs including: the JackRabbit OG2, the JackRabbit OG2 Pro, the JackRabbit XG, the JackRabbit XG Pro, the JackRabbit MG Doble, the JackRabbit MG Renegade and 30+ accessories through their website, shipping directly to customers across the United States, Canada, and Puerto Rico. JackRabbit also sells through a growing network of 200+ dealers including DICK'S Sporting Goods, Public Lands, DICK'S House of Sports, SCHEELS, and Specialized.

JackRabbit has a small but mighty team of passionate electric mobility enthusiasts.
They believe in creating useful, high-quality products with everything you need to get from point A to point B, and none of the bulk you don't need. Their mission is to make mobility make sense for everyone: electric-powered, ultra-portable, and accessible design with refined engineering - and a ton of fun along the way. Follow the micro movement @jackrabbit.bike or visit www.jackrabbit.bike

Contact:
Emily Johnson
858-243-3678

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/jackrabbit-expands-retail-footprint-with-dicks-sporting-goods-public-lands-and-dicks-house-of-sport-302555173.html

SOURCE JackRabbit Mobility, Inc.

data.title: Kay Properties Launches The “1031 DST Digest” Magazine
data.description:
Kay Properties Launches The “1031 DST Digest” Magazine

Kay Properties Founder and CEO, Dwight Kay, expands its investor education platform with the 1031 DST Digest, a magazine dedicated to advanced DST investment strategies, general 1031 exchange background knowledge, 721 exchange UPREITs, and real-life case studies from actual investors.

Kay Properties & Investments, a national leader in Delaware Statutory Trust (DST) investments and 1031 exchange strategies, has launched a new investor resource called the “1031 DST Digest.” The magazine is designed to deliver practical guidance and in-depth strategies for accredited investors exploring DST and 1031 exchange opportunities.

The 1031 DST Digest builds on Kay Properties’ long-standing mission to provide premier investor education. It features exclusive articles, expert insights, and real-world case studies to help investors make informed decisions about complex DST and 1031 concepts. Combined with the firm’s other library of educational tools, the recently published glossy magazine makes an excellent addition to the “Delaware Statutory Trust (DST) 1031 Exchange Toolkit.”

Kay Properties has completed nearly 10,000 DST, 1031, and 721 exchange UPREIT transactions and has received hundreds of positive testimonials from satisfied investors nationwide, and holds an A+ Better Business Bureau rating.*

Dwight Kay, Founder and CEO of Kay Properties, called the launch of this publication a wonderful compendium of information for 1031 exchange and DST 721 UPREIT investors.

“We received dozens of investor requests before the magazine even launched, showing the strong demand for educational tools in the 1031 DST space,” said Kay. “This publication can potentially bridge the gap between confusion and clarity.”

“There’s no doubt the 1031 DST Digest should be a go-to resource for any real estate investor considering a 1031 exchange, Delaware Statutory Trust or 721 exchange UPREIT,” said Kay Properties President Chay Lapin.

Investors can request a complimentary subscription by visiting www.1031dstdigest.com.

Kay Properties helps investors choose 1031 exchange investments that help them focus on what they truly love in life, whether that be their children, grandkids, other businesses, travel and hobbies. The company has helped 1031 exchange investors for nearly two decades exchange into nearly 10,000 1031 exchange investments.

* This material is not tax or legal advice. Please consult your CPA/attorney for guidance. Past performance does not guarantee or indicate the likelihood of future results. Diversification does not guarantee returns and does not protect against loss. Potential cash flow, potential returns and potential appreciation are not guaranteed. There is a risk of loss of the entire investment principal. Please read the Private Placement Memorandum (PPM) for the offerings business plan and risk factors before investing. Securities are offered through FNEX Capital LLC member FINRA, SIPC.

For more information on the new publication and other resources, please visit the Kay Properties and Investments website https://www.kpi1031.com/.

Phone: 424.436.3526
Email: vincent.aviani@kpi1031.com

data.title: SHAREHOLDER INVESTIGATION: Halper Sadeh LLC Investigates HBI, BFIN, PBBK, SONN on Behalf of Shareholders
data.description:
SHAREHOLDER INVESTIGATION: Halper Sadeh LLC Investigates HBI, BFIN, PBBK, SONN on Behalf of Shareholders

SHAREHOLDER INVESTIGATION: Halper Sadeh LLC Investigates HBI, BFIN, PBBK, SONN on Behalf of Shareholders

PR Newswire

NEW YORK, Sept. 12, 2025

NEW YORK, Sept. 12, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Halper Sadeh LLC, an investor rights law firm, is investigating the following companies for potential violations of the federal securities laws and/or breaches of fiduciary duties to shareholders relating to:

HanesBrands Inc. (NYSE: HBI)'s  sale to Gildan Activewear Inc. for 0.102 common shares of Gildan and $0.80 in cash for each share of HanesBrands common stock. If you are a HanesBrands shareholder, click here to learn more about your rights and options.

BankFinancial Corporation (NASDAQ: BFIN)'s  sale to First Financial Bancorp for 0.48 of a share of First Financial common stock per share of BankFinancial. If you are a BankFinancial shareholder, click here to learn more about your legal rights and options.

PB Bankshares, Inc. (NASDAQ: PBBK)'s sale to Norwood Financial Corp. Under the terms of the proposed transaction, PB Bankshares' shareholders will have the option to elect to receive either 0.7850 shares of Norwood common stock or $19.75 in cash for each common share of PB Bankshares they own. If you are a PB Bankshares shareholder, click here to learn more about your legal rights and options.

Sonnet BioTherapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ: SONN)'s  merger with Rorschach I LLC. Upon completion of the proposed transaction, Sonnet shareholders will own approximately 1% of the combined company. If you are a Sonnet shareholder, click here to learn more about your legal rights and options.

Halper Sadeh LLC may seek increased consideration for shareholders, additional disclosures and information concerning the proposed transaction, or other relief and benefits on behalf of shareholders. We would handle the action on a contingent fee basis, whereby you would not be responsible for out-of-pocket payment of our legal fees or expenses.

Shareholders are encouraged to contact the firm free of charge to discuss their legal rights and options. Please call Daniel Sadeh or Zachary Halper at (212) 763-0060 or email sadeh@halpersadeh.com or zhalper@halpersadeh.com.

Halper Sadeh LLC represents investors all over the world who have fallen victim to securities fraud and corporate misconduct. Our attorneys have been instrumental in implementing corporate reforms and recovering millions of dollars on behalf of defrauded investors.

Attorney Advertising. Prior results do not guarantee a similar outcome.

Contact Information:
Halper Sadeh LLC
Daniel Sadeh, Esq.
Zachary Halper, Esq.
(212) 763-0060
sadeh@halpersadeh.com
zhalper@halpersadeh.com
https://www.halpersadeh.com
